title: Root the Box
module_title: Linux Privilege Escalation Without Kernel Exploits
learning_objectives: Identify and exploit SUID binaries for privilege escalation
Find and hijack vulnerable cron jobs
Provide remediation guidance for each vector
scenario: Three Linux machines, three different PrivEsc vulnerabilities. Low-privilege SSH access. Escalate to root on all three — no kernel exploits allowed.

scenario_context.setting: Three Linux systems with common misconfigurations
scenario_context.challenge: Exploit configuration weaknesses to achieve root without kernel exploits
scenario_context.relevance: Most real Linux PrivEsc relies on misconfigurations, not kernel exploits

subtasks.title: Machine 1: SUID Escalation
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Find SUID binaries using 'find / -perm -4000'. Check GTFOBins for exploitation techniques. Exploit SUID binary to gain root shell.

subtasks.title: Machine 2: Cron Job Hijacking
subtasks.description: Find and exploit vulnerable cron job.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Check /etc/crontab and cron.d for writable scripts. Identify cron jobs run by root. Modify or hijack script. Wait for execution or force trigger.
subtasks.evidence_required: Root shell evidence + cron exploit documentation

subtasks.title: Machine 3: Sudo Misconfiguration
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Check sudo -l for commands user can run without password. Find GTFOBins bypass for allowed command. Exploit to escalate.
subtasks.evidence_required: Root shell evidence + sudo exploit path documentation

subtasks.title: Technique Comparison
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Create matrix comparing: attack vector, detection difficulty, reliability, remediation, applicability. Analysis of strengths/weaknesses.

subtasks.title: Remediation Guide
subtasks.detailed_guidance: For each vulnerability type, provide specific remediation steps and hardening commands. Document defensive controls.

reflection_prompt: Which privilege escalation vector was most common in your assessments? How would you prioritize remediation?

quiz.question: Why are SUID binaries exploitable for privilege escalation?
options: They run with owner's permissions (often root)
quiz.explanation: SUID binaries run with the file owner's permissions. If owned by root and exploitable, they lead to root privilege escalation.

quiz.question: What makes cron job hijacking effective for privilege escalation?
options: Cron jobs are visible to all users
Cron jobs run with owner's privileges (often root)
quiz.explanation: If a cron job script is writable by an unprivileged user and runs as root, modifying the script provides automatic root-level command execution.

portfolio_artifacts: Machine 1: SUID exploitation proof and methodology
Machine 2: Cron hijacking proof and documentation
Machine 3: Sudo escalation proof and exploit path
mentor_notes: Assess technical success (3/3 escalations required) and depth of understanding. Comparison matrix should show analytical thinking. Remediation guide quality indicates system hardening knowledge. No kernel exploits allowed.
